avoid: Do not promote this candidate as an optimization; it is effectively the baseline.
Do not write a custom full GEMM for W @ X because the main d x d GEMM should remain cuBLAS-backed.
Do not materialize B.T as a separate tensor.
Do not use a U layout of [16,d] with leading dimension 16; prefer U shape {d,16} with ldc=d.
Do not use opA=T/opB=T in SGEMM1 or SGEMM3 unless the full row-major/cuBLAS mapping has been carefully proven correct.
Do not hardcode a single hidden dimension because d is runtime-selected in [3584, 4608].
Do not pursue result caching unless the benchmark explicitly defines repeated identical inputs as the target workload.
evidence: Correctness passes exactly for all tested d values: 3584, 4096, and 4608.
Aggregate speedup is 0.9997x, effectively identical to the PyTorch/ATen baseline.
Per-shape speedups are all approximately 1.0x: 0.9997x at d=3584, 0.9995x at d=4096, and 0.9999x at d=4608.
Latency matches torch_ms almost exactly, indicating this candidate is just the ATen reference path rather than a meaningful optimized implementation.
Profile labels the bottleneck as multiple ATen GEMM and add launches.
recommended_actions: Replace the ATen reference implementation with a pure cuBLAS three-SGEMM implementation.
Keep W @ X as a direct cuBLAS SGEMM; do not attempt a custom full d x d GEMM.
Use the exact reference three-SGEMM mapping: SGEMM1 computes Y = X @ W with opA=N, opB=N, m=d, n=d, k=d, producing Y.
Compute the rank-16 intermediate U with shape {d,16} using SGEMM2: opA=N, opB=T with A=X and B=B, m=d, n=16, k=d, ldc=d.
Accumulate the low-rank term directly into Y using SGEMM3: opA=N, opB=N with A=U and B=A, m=d, n=d, k=16, beta=1.
Avoid explicit B.T materialization; use cuBLAS transpose flags instead.
Validate the pure cuBLAS path first before exploring handwritten rank-16 kernels.
If a future cuBLAS candidate is incorrect, first inspect row-major/column-major interpretation, op flags, m/n/k dimensions, leading dimensions, and alpha/beta settings.
